_about: Source-of-truth key-results for the Sprint 11 portfolio package (README, validation write-up, arkaneworks case study). EXTRACTED from committed artifacts by tools/build_key_results.py — do not hand-edit; regenerate. Cardinal rule 5: figures are sourced, never retyped.
as_of_sha_note: aether commit whose committed artifacts these figures were read from; deliverables cite 'as of aether <sha>'. The figures move only when an artifact moves.

deployment.integrity: tools/verify_deployment.py — GREEN at the pinned SHA

goturdepe.name: Turkmenistan Goturdepe / Barsagelmez O&G methane plume

permian.name: Permian / Carlsbad NM O&G methane plume

carried_goturdepe_mf_bias_does_not_trans.display: 1.46× measured on Goturdepe; flips to 0.96× here (does NOT transfer)

criterion.display: pre-registered V1: ERA5 peak 46.68 °C vs max station 45.0 °C, within ±2.5 K → pass=True
pre-registered V3: ERA5 +4.501 K vs ERA5-independent IMD +4.307 K (|Δ| 0.194 K < 1.0), pattern r 0.874 → pass=True
pre-registered V4a FAILED (pass=False): ERA5 26 d vs IMD 7 d — criterion-edge fragility is the finding
pre-registered V4b FAILED (pass=False): ERA5 887,700 vs IMD 606,300 km² common-grid, rel diff 0.464 > 0.30

v2_era5_station_consistency_NOT_CLAIMED.display: V2 FAILED (pass=False): pooled r 0.728 < 0.85 (bias 0.37 K and RMSD 1.05 K were within threshold) — consistency permanently not claimed

caveat.display: Terra snapshot at ~10.7 h local — never a daily maximum; Aqua absent for the window

value.display: -0.77 ± 0.80 K (NEGATIVE daytime surface UHI — urban cool island, counter to the prior)

confidence_cap.value: Warming-contributor tiers are capped at moderate: diagnostics establish presence and rarity of co-varying factors but cannot causally separate them without counterfactual experiments (out of scope). HIGH is reserved and unearned.
confidence_cap.display: warming-contributor tiers capped at moderate; HIGH reserved

f1_ridge.display: F1 persistent synoptic ridge: heuristic score 1.00 (CAPPED to moderate); z500 +61.6 m, 100th pct of 30 windows, 10/10 days above pooled p90

f5_urban_counter_evidence.display: F5 urban fabric is COUNTER_EVIDENCE (role=counter_evidence, score 0.00) — the negative daytime UHI argues AGAINST the urban-heat prior

scoring_disclaimer.value: Scores are documented heuristics over computed diagnostics — weighted presence/rarity/persistence indices, NOT calibrated probabilities and NOT contribution fractions. Use the tiers and the headline, not the decimals.
scoring_disclaimer.display: scores are documented heuristics, NOT calibrated probabilities or contribution fractions

citations.citation: Thorpe, A. K., et al. (2023). Attribution of individual methane and carbon dioxide emission sources using EMIT observations from space. Science Advances, 9(46), eadh2391.
NASA LP DAAC. EMIT L2B Estimated Methane Plume Complexes 60 m V002. Plume complex EMIT_L2B_CH4PLM_002_20220815T042838_000494 corresponds to this granule.
NASA LP DAAC. EMIT L2B Methane Enhancement Data 60 m V002. The raster EMIT_L2B_CH4ENH_002_20220815T042838_2222703_003 is used as the Stage A spatial-agreement reference.

Gordon, I. E., et al. (2022). The HITRAN2020 molecular spectroscopic database. Journal of Quantitative Spectroscopy and Radiative Transfer, 277, 107949. Line-by-line CH4 source for our INDEPENDENTLY GENERATED matched-filter unit absorption spectrum k (the operational retrieval since the Sprint 6 migration).

Kochanov, R. V., et al. (2016). HITRAN Application Programming Interface and efficient spectroscopic tools (HAPI). Journal of Quantitative Spectroscopy and Radiative Transfer, 177, 15-30. Computes the Voigt cross-sections used to build our independent k.

NASA EMIT-Data-Resources tutorial Generating_Methane_Spectral_Fingerprint.ipynb. Ships the per-granule methane target spectrum file emit20220815t042838_ch4_target, used as a SPECTRAL-SHAPE CROSS-CHECK ONLY (Pearson r = 0.993 vs our independent HITRAN2020 k) — NOT a pipeline input since the Sprint 6 migration. Our matched filter no longer reads this file.

Srivastava, A., Kumar, N., & Mohapatra, M. (2024). Unprecedented hot weather diagnosis in India during March-April 2022. MAUSAM, 75(2), 551-558. (India Meteorological Department; documents the IMD heat-wave definition, the 2022 March/April records, and major spells 11-21 Mar, 26-31 Mar, 25-30 Apr.)

Zachariah, M., Arulalan, T., AchutaRao, K., Saeed, F., Jha, R., Dhasmana, M. K., Mondal, A., Bonnet, R., Vautard, R., Philip, S., et al. (2023). Attribution of 2022 early-spring heatwave in India and Pakistan to climate change: lessons in assessing vulnerability and preparedness in reducing impacts. Environmental Research: Climate, 2(4), 045005. (Peer-reviewed event definition: March-April 2022 daily-maximum temperature over north Indian plains and southern Pakistan; ~1-in-100-yr; ~30x likelier and ~1 K hotter than preindustrial.)
World Weather Attribution (2022, May). Climate change made devastating early heat in India and Pakistan 30 times more likely. (Rapid attribution report preceding the peer-reviewed paper.)
NASA Earth Observatory (2022, April 29). Early Season Heat Waves Strike India. (Dated station values for the late-April wave; GEOS-5 2 m air temperature map for 2022-04-27.)

ESA (2022, April). Heatwave across India — Copernicus Sentinel-3 SLSTR land-surface temperature, 2022-04-29 10:30 local. LST exceeding 60 degC in places while air temperatures were 43-46 degC — a citable, official articulation of the LST-vs-air-temperature distinction (cardinal rule 2). Citation/context only; Sentinel-3 is not an Aether data source.

BusinessToday (2022, April 30). Northwest, central India experience hottest April in 122 years: IMD. (Secondary press reporting of the IMD Director General's statement: NW India April 2022 average maximum 35.9 degC, central India 37.78 degC, 10-20 heat-wave days vs a normal of 1-5. Used because IMD's primary press-release URLs now 404; status stated, not hidden.)
NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory. (2022, October 25). Methane 'Super-Emitters' Mapped by NASA's New Earth Space Mission. Press release.
OGIM_v2.7 (Oil and Gas Infrastructure Mapping database)
Duren, R. M., et al. (2019). California's methane super-emitters. Nature 575.
Cusworth, D. H., et al. (2021). Intermittency of large methane emitters in the Permian Basin. Environ. Sci. Technol. Lett.
